text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY - END-OF-TREATMENT ASSESSMENT
ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Gynecologic Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Follow-up / End-of-Chemotherapy Evaluation

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Delacroix is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman with stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, 5382insC) presenting today for end-of-chemotherapy assessment following completion of 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Delacroix was diagnosed approximately 9 months ago with FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0) high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary, confirmed on peritoneal biopsy at diagnostic laparoscopy. Germline testing identified a pathogenic BRCA1 founder variant (c.5266dupC, 5382insC). She underwent primary debulking surgery achieving complete gross resection (R0), followed by adjuvant carboplatin AUC 6 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 every 21 days for 6 cycles. Her pre-operative CA-125 was 1,847 U/mL; it fell to 342 U/mL post-operatively and has continued to decline throughout treatment.

She completed cycle 6 approximately 3 weeks ago. Paclitaxel was dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 (dose level -1) starting with cycle 5 due to Grade 2-3 peripheral sensory neuropathy, including left-sided foot drop that had developed by that point. Carboplatin was maintained at AUC 6 throughout all 6 cycles. End-of-treatment CA-125, drawn last week, returned at 18 U/mL - within the normal range. PET-CT performed last week showed no FDG-avid disease anywhere.

Since completing her last cycle, she reports ongoing but slowly improving neuropathic symptoms. The left foot drop has improved since physical therapy was started. She denies new abdominal pain, bloating, early satiety, or change in bowel habits. She continues to feel fatigued but notes gradual improvement over the past two weeks. No fever, chills, or signs of infection.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue, Grade 1 at this time (improved from Grade 2 during active chemotherapy). No fever, no night sweats, no significant weight loss since completing treatment. Appetite gradually recovering.
- Neurological: Persistent bilateral distal sensory neuropathy, hands and feet. Left foot drop present but improved with physical therapy. Denies new falls. Reports difficulty with prolonged walking but is ambulatory.
- GI: No nausea or vomiting (last bout of chemotherapy-related nausea resolved). Normal bowel function. No constipation or diarrhea. No abdominal distension.
- GU: Denies urinary symptoms. Vaginal dryness consistent with surgical menopause - using topical lubricants.
- Hematologic: No bleeding, bruising, or petechiae. No epistaxis.
- Musculoskeletal: Arthralgias improved post-paclitaxel.
- Psychiatric: Reports heightened anxiety regarding outcome and potential recurrence. Engaged with support resources.
- All other systems reviewed and negative.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
- General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Thin. Complete scalp alopecia (expected, post-paclitaxel; beginning to regrow fine hair). Alert and oriented x3.
- Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, temperature 36.8°C, oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Weight 72 kg. BMI 26.4 kg/m2.
- HEENT: Anicteric sclerae. Oropharynx clear. No oral mucositis.
- Lymph nodes: No cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.
- Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
- Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion.
- Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses. Well-healed midline laparotomy scar without evidence of herniation or wound breakdown. No ascites detected on exam.
- Extremities: No peripheral edema. Neurologic exam of lower extremities demonstrates reduced sensation to light touch in bilateral feet and toes (stocking distribution). Mild residual left foot dorsiflexion weakness (improved from prior); patient ambulates with mild antalgic gait, no assistive device currently needed.
- Pelvic/Rectal: Deferred today given recent completed treatment and no clinical indication for immediate repeat exam.
- Performance Status: ECOG 1 (limited by residual neuropathy and fatigue, though both improving).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
- CA-125: 18 U/mL (normal; down from 1,847 U/mL at diagnosis and 342 U/mL post-operatively - excellent biochemical response with normalization)
- CBC:
  - Hemoglobin: 10.6 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia; stable, anticipated post-platinum/taxane)
  - Platelets: 189,000/uL (within normal limits, recovering well from nadir)
  - ANC: 2,200/uL (within normal limits)
  - WBC: 4,800/uL
- BMP:
  - Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (stable; no evidence of carboplatin nephrotoxicity)
  - BUN: 14 mg/dL
  - Electrolytes: Sodium 139, Potassium 4.0, Chloride 102, Bicarb 24 - all within normal limits
  - Magnesium: 1.8 mEq/L (mildly low; supplementation recommended)
- LFTs: ALT 22, AST 18, Alk Phos 78, Total Bilirubin 0.6 - all within normal limits

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
PET-CT (performed within the past week): No FDG-avid disease identified. No abnormal metabolic activity in the pelvis, abdomen, chest, or elsewhere. Background SUVmax of post-surgical pelvic tissue 1.4. No mediastinal, hilar, or supraclavicular uptake.

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis (concurrent): No ascites. No peritoneal implants or nodular thickening. No pelvic or para-aortic lymphadenopathy. No hepatic lesions. Post-surgical changes in pelvis stable and expected. No pleural effusion.

Overall: Complete metabolic and radiographic response to primary debulking surgery followed by 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel. No evidence of disease.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. FIGO Stage IIIC High-Grade Serous Ovarian Carcinoma, Germline BRCA1-Mutant (5382insC), HRD-Positive - COMPLETE CLINICAL, BIOCHEMICAL, AND RADIOGRAPHIC RESPONSE.
   - CA-125 normalized at 18 U/mL (from 1,847 U/mL). PET-CT negative. Complete radiographic clearance. This is an excellent outcome following R0 debulking and 6 cycles of platinum-based chemotherapy.
   - Discussed response assessment with patient and husband today in detail. Congratulated the patient on achieving complete remission. Emphasized that while this is a significant milestone, the natural history of stage IIIC HGSOC carries a meaningful recurrence risk, which is the rationale for maintenance therapy.

2. MAINTENANCE THERAPY - PARP INHIBITOR:
   - Given: (a) germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC), (b) HRD-positive tumor (HRD score 56, above threshold), and (c) complete response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy - patient is an ideal candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy with a well-established survival benefit.
   - Reviewed maintenance therapy options in detail:
     * Niraparib (Zejula): FDA-approved for first-line maintenance regardless of BRCA/HRD status, with particularly robust benefit in BRCA-mutant and HRD-positive patients. Once-daily oral dosing. PRIMA trial data reviewed - HRD-positive subgroup demonstrated marked improvement in progression-free survival with niraparib vs. placebo. Individualized starting dose (ISD) protocol available based on weight and platelet count to mitigate hematologic toxicity.
     * Olaparib (Lynparza): FDA-approved for BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer, first-line maintenance (SOLO-1 trial). Twice-daily dosing, 150 mg BID. Also highly effective.
   - Patient elected niraparib based on the convenience of once-daily dosing. This preference is reasonable and well-supported by evidence.
   - Dosing: Patient weighs 72 kg and platelets today are 189,000/uL. Per the PRIMA individualized starting dose protocol, patients weighing <77 kg OR with platelets <150,000/uL at baseline are started at 200 mg daily rather than the standard 300 mg. Patient's weight is below the 77 kg threshold. I am electing to start at 200 mg once daily (ISD) to reduce the risk of early hematologic toxicity, particularly thrombocytopenia and anemia, which are the most common dose-limiting adverse effects of niraparib.
   - Reviewed toxicity profile with patient: nausea (often manageable with evening dosing and antiemetics), fatigue, thrombocytopenia, anemia, neutropenia, rare risk of MDS/AML (<1%), hypertension, headache, and insomnia.
   - Patient verbalized understanding and expressed willingness to proceed. Written consent obtained per institutional policy. Prescription for Zejula (niraparib) 200 mg daily to be called in.
   - CBC monitoring: Weekly CBC x 4 weeks after initiation, then monthly thereafter while on maintenance. Instruct patient to call or come in for any bleeding, easy bruising, or unusual fatigue.
   - Planned duration: Until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. No predetermined stopping point per current guidelines.

3. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (Grade 3, cumulative paclitaxel-related, improving):
   - Documented Grade 3 neuropathy by end of chemo (foot drop, fine motor difficulty). Now improving to approximately Grade 2 with physical therapy. Left foot dorsiflexion improved significantly.
   - Continue physical therapy as currently prescribed. Re-evaluate neuropathy at next visit.
   - EMG/nerve conduction study previously ordered - results to be reviewed at upcoming neurology follow-up.
   - Neuropathy not expected to be significantly worsened by niraparib.

4. ANEMIA (Grade 1, post-chemotherapy):
   - Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL. Asymptomatic. No transfusion indicated. Will monitor with serial CBC as part of niraparib monitoring plan. Iron studies not yet obtained - will check ferritin, iron, TIBC at next visit to rule out iron deficiency as a contributing/correctable cause.

5. HYPOMAGNESEMIA (mild):
   - Magnesium 1.8 mEq/L. Start oral magnesium supplementation (magnesium oxide 400 mg daily). Recheck at next visit.

6. MENOPAUSAL SYMPTOMS (surgical/natural menopause):
   - Hot flashes and vaginal dryness previously noted. Non-hormonal management with venlafaxine 37.5 mg daily to be discussed at next visit if not already initiated. Topical vaginal lubricants continued. Hormone replacement therapy not recommended given current data in BRCA1 carriers with gynecologic malignancy on active maintenance therapy.

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT:
   - Patient endorses anxiety about recurrence. Reinforced that complete remission is achieved and maintenance therapy is aimed at sustaining this. Referral to oncology social work placed. Ovarian cancer survivor support group information provided (NOCC, OCRA resources).

8. BRCA1 GENETIC IMPLICATIONS:
   - Daughter confirmed to carry BRCA1 5382insC variant. Confirmed that she has been referred to the high-risk program. Patient reminded to ensure son (if applicable) is aware of paternal BRCA1 transmission risk, though male BRCA1 carriers have lower but non-negligible cancer risk (breast, prostate, pancreatic).
   - Breast surveillance: Annual mammography and breast MRI per NCCN BRCA1 surveillance guidelines. Confirm last breast MRI timing at next visit.

RETURN TO CLINIC:
- 4 weeks from today for CBC check and tolerance assessment of niraparib. Sooner if significant bleeding, thrombocytopenia symptoms, severe nausea, or any concerning new symptoms.

Time spent with patient today: approximately 50 minutes (face-to-face counseling, examination, and care coordination). More than half of encounter time spent in counseling and coordination of care.

Dictated/Documented by: Attending Gynecologic Oncologist
